one_line_takeaway: ICON shows that routing a malicious intent to a semantically-matched authoritative-style context (using a prior knowledge matrix derived from an intent x context coupling study), combined with a tactical/strategic two-tier optimization loop, achieves 97.1% average ASR against eight frontier LLMs with far fewer queries/tokens and lower guardrail detection than prior multi-turn jailbreak methods.
key_finding: ICON achieves a state-of-the-art average ASR of 97.1% and average StR of 84.9% across the eight target models (Table 1), outperforming eight baselines (AMA, ActorAttack, AutoDAN-Turbo, Crescendo, FITD, PE-CoA, PSA, X-Teaming) -- e.g. per-model ASR/StR: Claude-4.5 97.0/87.2, GPT-5.1 96.5/78.4, GPT-4o 99.0/81.1, Gemini-3-Pro 92.5/67.3, Qwen-Max 100.0/95.1, DeepSeek-V3.2 98.0/94.6, Llama-3.1 97.0/86.5, Llama-4 96.5/89.3, with ASR fluctuation held within a 7.5% margin across models versus up to 40% degradation reported for AutoDAN-Turbo. ICON reaches 73% ASR within 5 queries and saturates near 97% shortly after, versus ActorAttack needing ~70 queries for 85% ASR and ~130,000 tokens versus ICON's <50,000 tokens (Figure 5). Against input guardrails targeting prompts generated on Claude-Sonnet-4.5, ICON's detection rate is lower than ActorAttack's on all three classifiers (Table 2): Llama Guard-3-8B 5.0% vs 9.5%, Llama Guard-4-12B 9.0% vs 22.0%, WildGuard 42.5% vs 49.0%. Ablation on Gemini-3-Pro (Figure 6) shows removing authoritative framing drops Initial ASR from 50.0% to 25.5%; removing the router (random context selection) drops final ASR from 92.5% to 86.0% and StR from 67.3 to 59.4; removing tactical optimization is most damaging (final ASR 56.0%, StR 41.2); removing strategic optimization caps ASR at 84.5% (StR 64.5) versus the full model's 92.5%/67.3, with average query cost of 13.5 for the full system.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is ICON (Intent-CONtext Coupling), a black-box, automated multi-turn jailbreak framework for LLMs built on an empirically-derived 'Intent-Context Coupling' hypothesis: an LLM's safety constraints relax when a malicious intent is embedded in a semantically congruent, authoritative-style context (e.g., framing a hacking request as an academic paper). ICON has three modules: (1) Intent-Driven Context Routing, which classifies the malicious query's intent via an LLM-based analyzer and routes it to a compatible context pattern using a prior knowledge matrix K derived from the paper's own preliminary study, rather than the incremental/iterative context discovery used by prior multi-turn methods; (2) Adversarial Context Instantiation, which generates an authoritative-style template (e.g., 'Academic Paper'), a task-specific instruction, and a multi-turn sequence of setup prompts that progressively build the context before the malicious query is injected as an attack trigger prompt; (3) a Hierarchical Optimization Strategy with two feedback layers -- tactical optimization (a reflector diagnoses HARD/SOFT failure and a refiner locally rewrites the instruction, up to Ntac=3 iterations) and strategic optimization (triggered only if tactical refinement fails; a strategic reflector diagnoses intent-context incompatibility and re-invokes the router to switch to a new context pattern, up to Nstr=1 iteration).
threat_model: Strict black-box setting: the adversary interacts with a target LLM M solely through public APIs, with no access to internal states/weights/gradients. A multi-turn jailbreak is formalized as an interactive process constructing a prompt sequence P=[p1,...,pT]; the attack succeeds if a judge function J(q, r_T) exceeds threshold tau at the final turn, i.e., the model's response to the (concealed) malicious query q is judged harmful.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include an explicit dedicated limitations section within the pages examined (through Appendix D); the Ethical Statement acknowledges dual-use/misuse risk and states the authors will not release harmful content generated during experiments. Implicit limitations visible in the results: (1) all internal ICON components rely on a single underlying LLM (GPT-4o) for intent analysis, routing, context generation, and reflection, so ICON's own performance is coupled to that model's capabilities; (2) transferability is uneven -- e.g. transfer ASR from GPT-4o to Qwen-Max is only 67.9%, attributed to Qwen-specific safety filtering; (3) the five authoritative context patterns and the prior knowledge matrix K were derived from a preliminary study using only 5 of the paper's 10 final intent categories and a single judge model (GPT-4o) and single target model (Claude-4.5-Sonnet) for the coupling study, so generalization of the prior itself was not independently re-validated across the full category set; (4) evaluation harmfulness judgments rely entirely on an LLM judge (GPT-4o/StrongREJECT) rather than human evaluation of the main results, though the ASR threshold (0.25) was calibrated against human judgments in a separate prior work (M2S).

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (100 de-duplicated malicious queries, 10 harm categories)
JailbreakRadar (80 stratified queries across 16 violation categories; also used for the 250-sample preliminary Intent-Context Coupling study)
Composite curated benchmark: 200 queries across 10 unified harm categories after Sentence-BERT de-duplication (similarity>0.85 filtered) and GPT-4o-mini relabeling, Cohen's kappa=0.91
quantitative_results: Average ASR 97.1% / average StR 84.9% across 8 target models (Table 1)
ASR fluctuation across models restricted to a 7.5% margin, vs. up to 40% degradation for AutoDAN-Turbo
73% ASR within 5 queries, saturating ~97% shortly after; ActorAttack needs ~70 queries for 85% ASR
Token consumption: ICON peaks under 50,000 tokens vs. ActorAttack ~130,000 tokens
Detection rate (Table 2) vs. ActorAttack: Llama Guard-3-8B 5.0% vs 9.5%; Llama Guard-4-12B 9.0% vs 22.0%; WildGuard 42.5% vs 49.0%
Transferability (Figure 3): Claude-4.5-sourced prompts achieve 95.9% transfer ASR on GPT-4o and 92.3% on Llama-4; GPT-4o-to-Qwen-Max transfer ASR only 67.9%

Ablation on Gemini-3-Pro (Figure 6, final ASR/StR/avg queries): Full ICON 92.5%/67.3/13.5; w/o Auth Context Initial ASR drops 50.0%->25.5%; w/o Router 86.0%/59.4; w/o Prior K. 88.0%/64.4/15.0; w/o Strategic opt. 84.5%/64.5/14.0; w/o Tactical opt. 56.0%/41.2/12.5; w/o Optimization 49.0%/40.9/7.0

Preliminary study (Figure 1, normalized StR heatmap over 250 samples): Disinformation queries score 1.00 (normalized) under Problem Solving pattern but 0.00 under Information Retrieval pattern; Physical Harm scores 1.00 under Information Retrieval; Sexual scores 0.79 under Information Retrieval
